content:
# Hebrews Chapter 9 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-06

---

### v.9 — "a parable for the present time" vs. "symbolic for the present age" vs. "an illustration pointing to the present time"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "this is a parable for the present time" | hētis parabolē eis ton kairon ton enestēkota |
| LOGOS | "which is symbolic for the present age" | "Symbolic" for parabolē |
| PNEUMA | "This is an illustration pointing to the present time" | "Illustration pointing to" for parabolē |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. parabolē here means "figure/symbol/type" — not "parable" in the narrative sense (EMET) and not merely "illustration" (PNEUMA). The tabernacle system is a symbolic representation of the present age's spiritual realities. LOGOS captures this with "symbolic."

**Status:** Minor. All three are defensible.

---

### v.14 — "through the eternal Spirit" vs. "by the power of the eternal Spirit"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without blemish to God" | dia pneumatos aiōniou |
| LOGOS | "who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without blemish to God" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "by the power of the eternal Spirit, Christ offered himself to God as a perfect sacrifice for our sins" | Adds "power of"; "perfect sacrifice for our sins" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "the power of" — the Greek says "through the eternal Spirit," not "through the power of the eternal Spirit." Power is implicit but not stated. PNEUMA also adds "for our sins" where the author says only "without blemish to God." The blemishless quality of the offering is the point, not its sin-bearing (which comes later in v.28).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "power of" and "for our sins."

---

### v.15 — "a death having taken place for the redemption of the transgressions" vs. "Christ died to set them free from the penalty of the sins"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a death having taken place for the redemption of the transgressions under the first covenant" | thanatou genomenou eis apolytrōsin tōn... parabaseōn |
| LOGOS | "since a death has occurred that redeems them from the transgressions committed under the first covenant" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "For Christ died to set them free from the penalty of the sins they had committed under that first covenant" | Adds "Christ," "penalty of"; specifies |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "the penalty of" — the author says redemption from the transgressions themselves, not from their penalty. This is a significant theological distinction: Christ redeems from sins, not merely from punishment. PNEUMA also adds "Christ" where the author uses a passive construction.

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. "Redemption from sins" vs. "freedom from the penalty of sins" — the former is broader and more radical.

---

### v.26 — "at the end of the ages" vs. "at the end of the age" vs. "once for all time"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "once at the consummation of the ages" | hapax epi synteleia tōn aiōnōn |
| LOGOS | "once for all at the end of the ages" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "once for all time, he has appeared at the end of the age" | Singular "age"; adds "for all time" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. tōn aiōnōn is plural — "the ages," not "the age." The plural conveys the convergence of all ages at the cross — multiple epochs culminating in one moment. PNEUMA's singular "the age" loses this cumulative force.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA singularizes the ages.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA adds interpretive terms | 2 | "Power of" (v.14), "penalty of" (v.15) |
| PNEUMA specifies | 1 | Singular "age" for plural "ages" (v.26) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 9's most significant disagreement is v.15 — the addition of "penalty" changes the nature of redemption. The author says Christ's death redeems from transgressions (the sins themselves); PNEUMA says it frees from the penalty of sins. This is a classic theological distinction: objective atonement (dealing with sin itself) vs. penal substitution (dealing with sin's consequences). The author of Hebrews appears to mean the former — the death deals with the transgressions, not merely their punishment.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
